title: Who Wants to Be a Professor?
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/mary-townsend
text:
In Josef von Sternberg’s The Blue Angel, a professor is destroyed. Whence comes his destruction? He is not a good teacher, but we did not expect him to be; his days in German Hochschule are filled with tantrums over unruly students and the pronunciation of the English word the, difficult for German schoolboys who care not at all. But the professor’s real destruction is Marlene Dietrich, which is to say, eros incarnate; he finds himself drawn to her first to censure—in this tale, she is a cabaret singer par excellence, glittering darkly in Weimar—then to desire, then to marriage, then to clownish madness, and finally death: collapsed all alone in the Hochschule classroom, still in the costume of the clown.

But why does eros incarnate destroy the professor? It is because of the nature of professing. The plot of every good story about professors, through a kind of narrative ill-fortune, must turn on this point. The problem is persona, both the persona, and persona itself. This persona, the schoolchild’s nemesis, stands alone at one end of a room, on a dais if possible, and is by second nature divorced from others present. The architecture of almost every current classroom lends itself to this divorce. Whence comes this isolation, and to what is it dedicated? To nothing less than reine Wissenschaft, or as we might say, somewhat less dramatically, to knowledge; conceived of in perfection, conceived on a dais, and conceived, of course, perfectly wrong. 

The professor is by definition a person who half knows this, and half does not. He is, as Kierkegaard puts it, super-fine and objective; permanently divorced from himself, and willfully so. He must take on and so achieve the persona of someone who is not a person; for such is the guarantor of his claim to knowledge, the objective cast that even his very subjectivity embodies. In our age, the persona may study Marlene Dietrich for a living, or be a she, or be himself a seducer, but nevertheless must take on the persona; a sign of this is that you can catch him boasting on Twitter that he never reads for pleasure, Euripides’s self-denying Hippolytus in coat, tie, and jeans. 

It is impossible to overestimate our animus against this unholiest of tyrants. We hate him in part because of our fealty to precisely this sort of knowledge, our grudging sense—albeit, weakening at present—that one has to give at least that to him, that he nevertheless possesses it; yet we reserve the right to the contempt that goes hand in hand with this most grudging sort of respect. Why we put this knowledge on the dais in the first place is perhaps too long a story to tell. But it is the remove, the dais, the impossibly objective subject that is the point, and it is the reason for the destruction-story too. As our respect for knowledge dies, as the will to untruth, always teasing at the edges and now at the center of common consciousness, flares and rumbles, both sides of the story flicker a bit, both the one where knowledge triumphs and the clown-colored remainder. Yet nevertheless we still see the end of the story coming: For the plot of every professor story is the rest of the persona’s soul coming to murder him in wholly justified revenge; what was dishonored, abandoned, left alone too long, never fully absent, and more able than anyone else to find the right place for the knife.

But a certain unrest remains. Despite all this, despite the ever-shrinking size of the honor given the dais, the professor job no longer being plausibly upper class, and indeed, usually not even of the middle, despite the faith in the reine to the Wissenschaft being worm-eaten quite, people, many people, real ones, still desperately long to have this sort of job. Indeed, too many perish for the lack of just a little more of the persona, in the professing world without granted the being of it; I speak of our university laboring poor, the adjunct, the contract worker, the ever-visitor on their third temporary appointment. And yet, here, unmistakably, remains desire. Even amidst all the work the university still most definitely depends on them to do—just this one time more without too much pay—there is palpably a yearning. And if you look farther still, you will see it also among the almost-finished dissertations, the person still dreaming of the just-missed grad school, the whole motley ecosystem of academic and para-academic wish. What do they want? They too have had or are learning contempt for those more permanently on the dais. The contradiction is interesting, a true puzzle. For if you set this seething realm of desire next to the man who did get the last office standing, and catch him in the midst of what ought to be a confession but is for him a boast—that he never reads a book simply because anymore, simply for the joy of it, all wish on the one side and the death of wish on the other—something of our dilemma will, we can hope, become more plain. Something is deeply amiss, amidst our reading and thinking lives. For where will all this deferred desire go? Who will it murder next? Indeed, what selves are left to murder.…

I can almost remember the first time I noticed professors were a thing; it was with a kind of narrative misfortune, I think around age thirty-two, that I noticed how the lovely office was the man, and it was this that after all he had desired to be. The narrative misfortune was that I was already in school to ostensibly have some sort of job or other like him. I had managed to come into the academy crabwise, and it was not until my second turn in graduate school that I noticed. And whence had I managed to find this sort of dubious innocence for so long? To confess or explain, I think it was because up till then I’d gone to school with monks: real ones, in the manner of Dominican friars, some unfortunately being just what Stendhal and Nietzsche might lead one to expect, and some magnificently better; and before that, in school with lay ones, lay as-near-as-makes-no-difference to monks, if more Rabelaisian, like in Mary McCarthy’s The Groves of Academe, escapees from the Second World War and from ordinary academic life, caught still in persona but each one more magnificently idiosyncratic than the last. I do not think all of their stories ended well, but each ending was nevertheless distinct, and their foibles, gloriously left to grow as they would, gave to the apprenticeship a fundamentally different sort of color. Most had a fundamental ressentiment toward what they remembered of the academy; they felt and they knew they were doing something different; that much I had taken in so far.

But the generational problem, the civilizational problem, is always what happens next. As Socrates says, cantankerously, usually no one in the slightest cares about education, at all, and this is usually why the rest of everyone murders each other, in person. As I was to learn, many of my fellow youthful learners already knew all about the professor, a figure of their early life; the smallness and remoteness of the place I came from, first of all, had preserved me from this sort of inherited awareness. Kierkegaard’s story of the man who hears the misplaced sermon in praise of Abraham and goes home innocently to murder his son has a certain lyrical simplicity to it for me, and is precisely to me recognizable, for it is what I did when I first heard education praised for real. I think I took what is often a ceremonial sort of praise in its strictest and most literal sense. People said to read, so I read; Platonists, real ones, told me to desire and long for that which I wanted most to know, to learn to look for the smallest line of desire into the heart of what I most did not understand, and I believed them. And though it puzzled me still to see all the ways people remained far from their selves, unknown to themselves, these knowers, despite this best advice, all was well until graduation. 

It was like being kicked out of a garden, and I did not understand. I’d spent careful years learning, learning how to learn, and then I was kicked out, with no clear path backward or forward, except to attend the sorts of less Mary McCarthy–type schools that I’d usually heard described as a travesty of learning. I signed up for the schools in any case, but in a kind of puzzlement with a rumbling resolution; and the transference of learning for desire itself to learning as the fight to the death of persona against persona almost scared me off entirely. What I was experiencing was the unhappiness of desire, and a kind of betrayal, a betrayal of a deeper kind of learning, but also of the deep civilizational lack of a natural place, a second-natural place, for the kind of living I wanted to do. At twenty-three, I knew I wanted to keep learning: I did not want to become one of the unhappy ones I vaguely saw existing at the edges of my community, remembering books without the ability or courage to read them still, and fortunately-unfortunately I still did not know that professors existed. 

What I wanted was a monastery, but one without the usual sorts of vows (except for genteel poverty; that one is fine), and it seemed to me that something of a bait and switch had occurred. My college had asked for desire and for the dedication of desire from me, and I had given it to them; but in return, there was no small cell in which I could simply stay at will, no small labor in Rabelaisian still or hive to trade for it, and this was the strangest sort of homelessness I’ve ever experienced—well, except for one thing more. I hadn’t known if I would like teaching or be anything close to good at it; in the graduate school, I did and I was, and then it became clear at the very same time, at the moment of conception, that another unwilling exodus of a sharper kind was not just possible but very, very likely. It simply might not be possible to continue to teach. To teach, that is, and to afford things; one looks at the classroom and knows in one’s bones that high-end waitressing would be, monetarily, a better life. And yet the knowledge that you too are good at it, that you can make something vivid to someone else with as little tyranny as humanly can be managed, that you are part of cantankerous Socrates’s civilizational project—even the first taste of this is sweet, and yes, it is full of desire, and this is why, I think, so many make the choice to descend into poverty because of it.

But graduate school does not exist to inculcate a love of teaching. Such an aim is far too lowly, and graduate school wishes to cultivate ambition for the highest. But what is the highest? Graduate school is built around the insistence and enforcement of the persona of the professor as the truly learned man. His tyranny becomes your life in drips and drabs, should you be so lucky. You may go into academic life with the veriest hatred of it, but it is structured around the demandthat your desire for learning, for the small quiet cell, becomes your desire for the persona. No longer can you rest, until the lovely office is yours. This sort of schooling teases your ambition until your ambition is its ambition; and it asks your ambition to learn its contempt. It is the worst sort of bait and switch, this exchange of desire for persona, the exchange of desire for contempt for desire, and, knowing this, when you see that this is the truest pattern, it is hard to forgive the sort of professor who can still convince himself that lack of merit alone cuts off the-ones-who-don’t-deserve-to-be-there, that everyone poor and on a contract is vicious and comes to their poverty through vice alone. Yet so often is this contempt internalized that vast numbers of people at the edges learn to accept it as their fate. And how did all this happen? You wanted the monastery, remember: But the monastery turned out to be something different, something that expelled you precisely for the infinity of your desire.

In our patterns and civilization of learning, this is what we do with the desire of our young book-learners: We set the weird goal of being-a-professor in front of all sorts of manifold desires to know, and then tell desire to choose: Either bethe sort of person, through some kind of natural fiat, who is by heaven-given chance the sort good enough, moral enough, to become the persona, to become and be purely wise, or accept estrangement from the truest life of the mind forever. This is not an especially believable additional story I think, but the walls have ears, and I have heard floating across the hall words of professorial counsel to a young, desiring one, a soon-to-be BA, that by now he would already know if he wanted to be a professor; and since the youth did not know, he probably did not. Consider the oddity of this advice, and where it tends. If you already wanted to be the thing that has no desire, you would already know, becoming a being with never a moment of space between you and the being; no space, that is, for the distance of desire. This is preternaturally bad advice, and I must stress to you again, offered by someone whose time in the office is only economically possible by those who take his advice and fail, by the calculated impoverishment of those poor and foolish enough to teach on the small part-time salary for its own sake; they began in their hope for being, but something went amiss. America, as the poet says, why are your libraries full of tears, or should we already know?

I have a good job now, although I did not used to. What saved me before and after was reading for pleasure. Or, as I put it at the time, becoming a sort of priesthood of one: one evening’s coffee-shop resolution, Dupont Circle, tired out with discalced monks, and faced down with a five-dollar Second Story tome, a history of ancient Babylon from 1919, Virginia Woolf’s barren brazen beak at work so hardily, perhaps the work of the very soul she’d sat next to in the British Library the century before. I discovered that my mind was less arid, in Woolf’s sense, if I split the day between philosophy, ostensibly my subject of choice, and literature, reading philosophy first, when sharpest in the morning, and then resting in story in the afternoon. Sometimes I would read literature first thing in the morning, if I felt particularly unbalanced. Nineteenth-century literature seemed too frivolous at first—I was entranced by what Tacitus would call the old morality—so I went backwards, and cut the pages of my library’s copy of eighteenth-century Samuel Richardson’s Sir Charles Grandison, because Austen had liked Richardson; in between volumes, I found small nooks in the upper-story stacks in which to listen to Philip Glass and pine. Surrounded by monks, I was determined to be a monk and yet somehow with desire. As I quickly learned, however, the thing was that it had to be something of a secret. One August back, I told a friend I had spent the summer reading Herodotus, and got the answer, “What FOR?!”; several years later, to another colleague confessing about the Richardson, in the elevator she said, “Who ARE YOU?” The only answer to this is, someone very foolish, someone criminally naive, and someone, years later, lucky to have a job. Indeed it is hard to avoid the sense that, years later, with the academic freedom to tell you the tale, to tell you all at last about it, I’ve somehow gotten away with murder.

For let me tell you the truth, this is the only thing that is real: a lifetime of books read for pleasure, just because. It is the thing that is the joy of it, the careful preservation of the smallest thread of desire through youth, more youth, and drawing into age. It is nothing else but this that is of what the soul is made, and civilization too. We are made to be both reason and desire, and fortunately unfortunately the way past self-murder is nothing other than this. From whence did it become so difficult? Yet perhaps we can worry less about this question, and rather on what we would like to read and work on next. For such is the only way to be a citoyenne of the true human world, someone who partly escapes time, in just the way that humans do. Nietzsche on this point has always been helpful to me. Here is some of his better advice, distilled: Don’t read yourself to death in your thirties; don’t think that arid mastery is more important than finding the thread each time again; learn desire first and quest for mastery later; spend time outside your century, real weeks and days outside the problems of your own time the better to return, and, best of all, in April, who could read? Put it down and walk, rather. No word without a tree. 

My first teacher of Plato, Eva Brann, famously tried to address this by praise of the amateur: the merely lover who, playfully, amats, the one who is not a profession or a professional but someone whose seriousness comes from the serious-unseriousness of love. For what price would the professor have to pay, feel himself choked into paying, if he could ever admit the demotion to amateur? The idea is well chosen, and yet I think she is half right. The soul does want to know, not tamely or partially but even into its own extremity and beyond, and it is a serious thing to want this knowledge, however pure; somehow we want this, and though it is partly misguided, it can yet be the noble beginning to a life lived in living thought. It will always be worth the risk of self-murder.

But consider this: What else is the hope for the large language model, the hope for knowledge as future-public-gas-utility, except for the newest and more pure expression of the wish to murder the professor than has ever yet been seen? It is pure ressentiment, but for what? Do not look to robots to murder the professor, for he is already dead. Desire and Marlene Dietrich have been enough for him already. We have suffered under his tyranny, but the knowledge-fantasy he was claiming, the purity of his objectivism, was never long for this world. There can be new songs and new ways to seek the better sort of monastery. There is no need to kill the soul as well. For outsourcing the dream of objectivity to a pattern-repeating machine, like Roald Dahl’s Great Automatic Grammatizator, is just another way of dying, the same death we already have. What would it be like to live instead? To look for wisdom, we must first desire; and no one, no one at all, will ever know the answer to that.
